content.problemStatement:
Mike is fond of collecting stamps. He has a lot of rare and unusual ones in his collection. Unfortunately, a few days ago he was fired from his well-paid job.

But he doesn't complain about that, he acts! That's why Mike picked up **N** stamps from his collection and is going to sell them. He has already placed an announcement on the Internet and got **M** offers. Each offer can be described as a set of stamps, that the buyer wants to buy. Now Mike needs your help to choose a set of offers, that he should accept. 

He can't accept offers partially. Also, as far as Mike has the only copy of each stamp, he can sell one stamp to at most one buyer.

Of course, Mike wants to maximize the number of accepted offers. Help him!
content.inputFormat: First line contains two integers N and M, denoting the number of stamps and the number of offers. Each of the next M lines contains the description of an offer in the format Ki Ai,1 Ai,2 ..., Ai,Ki where Ki is the number of stamps the i-th buyer wants to buy and Ai is the list of stamps sorted in ascending order.
content.outputFormat: The output should contain the only integer, denoting the maximal number of the offers that Mike can accept.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Mike should accept the first and the third offer.
Mike can accept all four offers.
Mike can accept all three offers.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a backtracking approach. We try to accept each offer one by one and check if it is possible to accept the current offer without violating the condition that no stamp can be sold to more than one buyer. If it is possible, we recursively try to accept the next offer. If it is not possible, we backtrack and try not to accept the current offer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(2^M) in the worst case, where M is the number of offers. However, the actual time complexity is much less than this because we prune the search space by checking if it is possible to accept the current offer before recursively trying to accept the next offer.
